transcript: Yeah, I've done this before. There was a situation at work where I needed to get someone on board with something and they didn't report to me. I just kind of talked to them and explained my thinking and eventually they agreed. It was fine, the project worked out.
strengths: Directly acknowledges the constraint of no authority
gaps: No STAR structure — no Situation, Task, Action, or Result
Zero specificity — no role, project, person, or outcome named
How the influence worked is entirely absent
one_specific_improvement: Name the context: add one sentence for your role, one for the project, and one for what the other person was doing before you describe what you did.

transcript: So there was a project I was working on and one of my colleagues wasn't really keeping up with their part of it. They didn't work for me or anything, so I couldn't just tell them what to do. I went and had a conversation with them about it. I explained that the project was important and that we needed to stay on track. They were receptive and things got better after that. The project ended up finishing on time which was good. I think the key was just being direct but not confrontational about it.
strengths: Adds a basic narrative arc — before and after the conversation
Correctly identifies being direct but not confrontational as the approach
gaps: Still no STAR structure — everything is one undifferentiated block
Specificity unchanged from attempt 1 — no names, numbers, roles, or outcomes
The insight in the final sentence is stated but not earned by the story
one_specific_improvement: Break your answer into two explicit parts: what the situation was and what you actually did in the conversation. Right now both are blended into a single vague paragraph.

transcript: At my previous company, I was working on a cross-functional project with a team of people who didn't report to me. One of the engineers on the team was consistently late with their deliverables, and it was starting to cause issues for the rest of us. Since I wasn't their manager, I decided to talk to them directly. I set up a meeting and explained the situation. I told them that the delays were affecting the team's ability to stay on schedule and that I wanted to understand what was going on. They said they had a lot on their plate and weren't sure how to prioritize. We talked about it and came up with a plan for them to communicate earlier when things were slipping. After that conversation, things improved and the project got back on track. I think the main takeaway is that sometimes you just need to have a direct conversation with people even when you don't have authority over them. People are usually pretty reasonable when you approach them respectfully.
strengths: Longer narrative with a clearer before-and-after arc
Includes the diagnostic move: wanting to understand what was going on
gaps: Specificity still at zero — no numbers, timelines, team roles, or named agreements
STAR structure is implicit at best — no clear Situation, Task, Action, Result delineation
Closing insight is generic: people are usually pretty reasonable adds nothing specific to this story
one_specific_improvement: Add one number anywhere in your answer — how many days late, how many sprints, how many tasks were blocked — and watch how much more credible the whole story becomes.

transcript: Situation: About eighteen months ago I was coordinating a product launch with a six-week timeline. The team was cross-functional — two engineers, a designer, a QA lead, and a marketing manager — none of whom reported to me. One senior engineer had missed their deliverables by two to three days across four consecutive sprints. Each slip was small, but the cumulative effect was eating into our QA buffer and threatening the launch date. Task: I needed to course-correct without formal authority and without damaging the working relationship. Escalating to management felt like the wrong first move — I wanted to try to solve it directly. Action: Before the conversation, I built a dependency map showing exactly which downstream tasks each delay had blocked and by how many days. I wasn't trying to build a case against them — I wanted us both to see the same picture. I asked them for an informal coffee. I started by asking how things were going on their end, which turned out to matter: they told me they were being pulled into P1 support incidents by another team with no advance notice. That completely reframed the conversation. Instead of addressing a performance issue, we were solving a capacity problem together. We agreed on two things: they'd block their mornings for project work, and they'd send me a heads-up by end of day if a deliverable was at risk. I also redistributed two lower-priority items to another team member to reduce their load. Result: Their delivery cadence improved immediately. We launched on time. And that engineer told me afterward that the way I approached it made them feel like a partner, not a problem. That feedback stuck with me — it told me something about how influence actually works when you don't have a title behind you.
strengths: Sudden and complete STAR structure — a clear breakthrough from the plateau
Excellent Action: dependency map, listening-first, reframes from performance issue to capacity problem
Compelling Result with qualitative impact: partner not a problem
gaps: Specificity good but not maximum — exact dates and buffer quantity not named
Conciseness could tighten: the opening Situation is slightly long relative to the rest
one_specific_improvement: Add one hard number to your Result — for example, how many consecutive sprints they delivered on time after the conversation — to match the specificity of your Action.

transcript: Situation: About eighteen months ago I was coordinating a high-visibility product launch — six weeks to go, VP-level eyes on it. The team spanned engineering, design, QA, and marketing. None of them reported to me. Around the four-week mark, I identified a pattern: one of the senior engineers had delivered two to three days late across four consecutive sprints. Individually, each slip felt manageable. But I'd tracked the cascade — those delays were blocking downstream tasks, compressing our testing window, and quietly putting the launch date at risk. Task: My job was to protect the timeline. My constraint was that I had no authority to direct this person's work. And my goal — beyond the project — was to solve this in a way that didn't damage the relationship or the team's dynamic. Escalating to their manager was a last resort. I wanted to try influence first. Action: I spent an hour before doing anything else. I mapped the dependencies — each delayed deliverable, which tasks it blocked, and the total buffer burned. I wanted to lead with shared data, not frustration. I asked the engineer for a coffee — invite said only wanted to sync on the project. I made a deliberate choice to listen first. I asked how things were going before I said anything about the delays. They told me they were being assigned to P1 support incidents by a different team lead with no advance warning and no capacity buffer. That changed everything. I shared the dependency map as shared context, then asked: What would make it easier to protect your time for this project? They drove most of the solution: blocking 9 to 12 for focused work, flagging slips by EOD. I absorbed two lower-priority deliverables by redistributing them, and set up a weekly 15-minute check-in. Result: The change was visible within the first week. When one external incident came up three weeks later, they flagged it two days early. We adjusted and launched on time. At the retrospective, the engineer said unprompted that the way I approached the conversation made them feel like a partner rather than a problem. The plateau I'd been stuck in with earlier attempts was describing what I did without understanding why it worked. The breakthrough: influence without authority is a posture, not a technique. You earn it by understanding someone's reality before trying to change their behavior.
strengths: Full STAR with maximum specificity: VP visibility, four sprints, P1 incidents, 9-12 blocks, EOD flag, two redistributed items, weekly check-in
Listening-first approach explicitly deliberate — made a deliberate choice to listen first
Closing insight names the plateau-to-breakthrough arc and elevates the answer to a leadership principle
gaps: Conciseness is the one remaining gap — the closing reflection extends slightly past the ideal length
one_specific_improvement: The closing insight is your strongest line — but it follows a Result that already closes well. Cut the sentence starting The plateau I'd been stuck in and lead directly with the principle: influence without authority is a posture, not a technique. Let it land as the final word.
